title: The Commission
description: The Bureau of Adjustment is hiring. Harrowgate station holds an imperial commission, the countryside is full of bandits, goblins, and dead that won't stay down — and a blade that earns its keep can build a name worth having. No crowd, no promoter. Just contracts, coin, and Standing.
introduction:
The road into Harrowgate is a bad one. Churned mud, a waystation with a cracked wardstone, and a notice-board that's been rained on so many times the ink has bled into the wood. You can still read it. BUREAU OF ADJUSTMENT — HARROWGATE STATION — COMMISSION HOLDERS — BLADES SOUGHT. No promises of glory. No seal of a noble house. Just the Empire's bureaucratic permission to hire people like you to do the work no one else will.

You came down from the mountain looking for something worth doing. The Bureau doesn't offer something worth doing. It offers a plank desk, a tired officer, and a ledger that will remember your name if you earn the entry. In the Sundered Reach, that's as close to honest work as it gets.

The dead are walking the back roads. The bandits have grown bold. The Capes won't touch any of it — skeletons are made by magic, but they don't need magic to put back down. They need a steady arm and a sharp edge.

Adjutant Vask is waiting. She's already buried more hired blades than she'd like. She will not pretend otherwise.

chapters.title: A Quiet Night at the Sparrow's Nest

aiContext:
THE COMMISSION is a three-part Bureau bounty adventure for Halden Marrick, levels 1–4. The Bureau of Adjustment holds an imperial commission at Harrowgate station — standing authority to hire mercenaries for contract work the Bureau's thin ranks cannot cover. The countryside is plagued by bandit raids, goblin skirmishers, and stray skeletons: remnants of old imperial magic the White Capes refuse to spend resources reclaiming. Skeletons are the reason the commission exists — they require no magic to destroy, only blades and nerve, which puts them squarely in the Bureau's hired-sword lane.

Three enemy families drive all combat: Bandits (human opportunists who use cover, can surrender for a capture bounty, have targetable leaders); Goblins (fast swarm skirmishers that rout when a fight turns, dangerous in numbers and open ground); Skeletons (magic-made, relentless, never flee or parley, vulnerable to bludgeoning, immune to poison). The core mechanic is Standing — a reputation currency earned by completing contracts. Standing raises Halden's rank, unlocks larger contracts, and ultimately gates the capstone assault. A multi-fight patrol is a press-your-luck gamble against limited field healing.

Key NPCs: Adjutant Renna Vask (Bureau officer, commission-holder, overworked and scrupulously fair, has buried more blades than she'd like and won't hide it); Captain Hesk (capstone antagonist, former Bureau sergeant turned deserter who organised the countryside's raiders into something dangerous — a dark mirror of the soldier Halden is trying to become); the roster (other hired blades at the station, color and occasional rivals or allies). Tone: grounded and grinding, not glamorous. The quiet thematic thread is that Halden came for glory and the Bureau offers only duty — and the man at the top of the capstone is what happens to a soldier who decided the work wasn't worth it.
